Name: OptimizedAdapterFusion
Title: Optimized Adapter Fusion for Efficient Inference in Large Language Models
Short Hypothesis: By fusing adapter layers with the main model's layers and optimizing kernel launches, we can significantly reduce inference latency while maintaining or improving model accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as AdaFuse and FlashInfer have explored techniques to optimize adapter-based models and attention mechanisms for efficient inference. AdaFuse focuses on reducing the overhead of dynamic adapter routing by employing a token-level pre-gating strategy and fused kernel optimization. FlashInfer addresses the efficiency of attention mechanisms by optimizing memory access and reducing redundancy. Our proposal builds on these ideas but specifically targets the fusion of adapter layers with the main model, focusing on kernel-level optimizations to minimize latency.
Abstract: Adapter-based fine-tuning has become a popular method for enhancing the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) without significantly increasing their size. However, the additional computational overhead of adapter layers can lead to increased inference latency. We propose Optimized Adapter Fusion (OAF), a framework that integrates adapter layers directly into the main model layers, thereby reducing the number of kernel launches and optimizing memory access patterns. OAF leverages custom CUDA kernels to perform fused operations, ensuring that the adapter computations are executed in a single, efficient pass. We evaluate OAF on popular LLMs and demonstrate significant reductions in inference latency while maintaining or improving model accuracy. Our approach is validated through detailed profiling and benchmarking on commodity GPUs, providing a practical and reproducible solution for efficient LLM inference.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the impact of fused adapter layers on inference latency and accuracy.
Experiments.Method: We will implement a fused adapter layer that integrates with the main model's layers using custom CUDA kernels. We will compare the latency and accuracy of the fused model against the baseline model with separate adapter layers. The evaluation will be performed on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, and we will use the Hugging Face Transformers library for model training and inference.
Experiments.Metrics: Inference latency (p50/p99), throughput (tokens per second), and model accuracy (F1 score, perplexity) on standard benchmarks such as GLUE and SuperGLUE.

Experiments.Description: Analyze the impact of kernel fusion on memory access patterns and compute efficiency.
Experiments.Method: We will use NVIDIA Nsight Systems and Nsight Compute to profile the fused and baseline models. We will measure key performance indicators such as achieved memory bandwidth, occupancy, and warp stall reasons. The goal is to identify the specific hardware-level optimizations that contribute to the latency reduction.
Experiments.Metrics: Achieved memory bandwidth, occupancy, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity.

Experiments.Description: Compare OAF with existing adapter optimization techniques.
Experiments.Method: We will benchmark OAF against state-of-the-art adapter optimization methods such as AdaFuse and FlashInfer. The comparison will focus on latency, throughput, and accuracy across a range of model sizes and input lengths. We will use the same hardware and software setup for a fair comparison.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of OAF may vary across different model architectures and input lengths. We will need to conduct extensive experiments to ensure that the benefits are consistent.
The custom CUDA kernels required for OAF may introduce additional complexity in the implementation and maintenance of the framework.
The optimization may not be as effective for models with very large adapter layers, as the additional computational load could outweigh the benefits of kernel fusion.
